methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $400M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $120M - $500M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Pano AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series B), post-money $500M+

private.name: Reverion
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series B), post-money $310M

private.name: CarbonCapture Inc.

private.name: Cemvita
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series B), post-money $120M

data.narrative:
Sage Geosystems, a Series B climate and energy company founded by Cindy Taff and Lance Cook, carries a current post-money valuation of $400M following $114M+ in total capital raised. The valuation assessment draws on a peer universe of 9 comparables—1 public (LNZA) and 8 private—within the same sector and stage, including Pano AI ($500M), Reverion ($310M), CarbonCapture Inc. ($300M), and Cemvita ($120M).

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-progression methodology, using a 1.9x multiplier typical for Series B advancement, implies a forward valuation of $760M. Peer-median analysis across the 4-company same-stage cohort yields $305M, with a range spanning $120M to $500M. These methods produced a triangulated range of $259M (low) to $874M (high), with a median of $532M.

The analysis supports a defensible valuation range between $500M and $650M, anchored to peer median but accounting for the company's capital efficiency and stage progression. Key uncertainties center on technology de-risking—the extent to which Sage's geothermal approach has demonstrated reproducibility at scale—and comparability drift, given the broad dispersion within the peer set ($120M to $500M). The current $400M valuation sits below the peer median and well below stage-progression guidance, suggesting either a discount to the cohort or material risk factors not reflected in public comparable multiples.
